summary: This link-roundup post covers several Mac OS X topics from mid-2003. Gruber highlights a hack restoring iTunes internet sharing after Apple removed it in 4.0.1, critiques Adobe's bloated new Acrobat Reader, amplifies Jonathan Rentzsch's exposé of the Finder's deceptive file extension hiding, documents another Finder inconsistency in List view, and shares a useful drag-and-drop tip. The thread running through these items is frustration with software that obscures information or behaves inconsistently, particularly Apple's own Finder.
key_points: A developer quickly hacked around Apple's removal of internet-wide music sharing in iTunes 4.0.1, illustrating the 'Steve giveth, Steve taketh away' dynamic
Adobe Reader 6 is criticized as bloated corporate software that betrays its purpose as a simple document viewer
The OS X Finder's file extension hiding is called out as actively deceptive, renaming files behind the user's back
Multiple Finder inconsistencies in List view show Apple failing to maintain the Mac's traditional interface polish
A useful hidden feature: Command-Tab works mid-drag-and-drop in OS X
money_quotes: Steve giveth, and Steve taketh away.
The product that should be a simple document viewer now looks and feels like it was from Microsoft.
Try to rename Foo.txt back to Foo.bar. You can't.
Oh sure, the Finder will list the file's name as Foo.bar again, but the Finder is lying to you.
Personally, I think these file suffix-hiding shenanigans are the single most un-Mac-like aspect of Mac OS X.

It's like a smelly deposit of dog shit on the carpet. Mac OS X users have learned to hold their breath and walk around it; whereas Mac OS 9 users gag, and demand to know why Apple hasn't cleaned this up.
key_insight: The OS X Finder's file extension hiding represents a fundamental betrayal of the Mac's tradition of honest, transparent user interfaces.
